// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 6 — WORKING WITH CLAUDE CODE

The pipeline has five stages. Each one is the output of a chapter.

Chapter 6 — Working with Claude Code — Chapter 3 read the dataset. You now know the attribute types, the number of observations, the analyst's question versus
NARRATION

The pipeline has five stages. Each one is the output of a chapter. Chapter 3 read the dataset. You now know the attribute types, the number of observations, the analyst's question versus the reader's question, and the comparison the chart needs to make explicit.

// SLIDE 02 — THE FOUR-MOVE STRUCTURE

THE FOUR-MOVE STRUCTURE.

Move 1: Show what you have.→Move 2: Say what you want.→Move 3: Constrain it.→Move 4: Ask for verification.
NARRATION

Every D3 prompt Claude Code receives has the same four moves. The moves come from Chapter 00. They are not a style; they are a protocol. Move 1: Show what you have. The dataset. Number of rows, column names, types, a sample of three to five rows. If the data is in a file Claude Code can read, name the path.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT CLAUDE CODE DOES AND DOES

WHAT CLAUDE CODE DOES AND DOES NOT DO WELL

D3 v7 syntax — d3.scaleLinear(), d3.select(), joins — Chart type — defaults to whatever is most familiar (pie, line)
Layout computation — Sankey, treemap, force-directed — Channel-to-attribute mappings — which variable goes on which chan
Responsive resize — <svg viewBox> with width 100% — Sort order — defaults to source-file order
Color scale implementation — sequential, diverging, categorical — Zero baseline — only added when explicitly requested
NARRATION

The labor in this pipeline is divided: Claude Code handles syntax and computation; you handle decisions and judgment. Understanding where the line is keeps the pipeline working. Claude Code is reliable for valid D3 v7 syntax. It is reliable for layout computation , Sankey flows, treemap nesting, force-directed simulations , via D3's layout primitives. It handles responsive resize via window event listeners. It generates accessibility metadata when asked. It implements color scales using D3's interpolators. It produces correct tick formatting.

// SLIDE 04 — THE MBTA LESSON

THE MBTA LESSON.

Get a working chart fast.→Iterate on the artifact, not the specification.→One concern per iteration.→The rendered chart is the truth.
NARRATION

Mike Barry and Brian Card built a complete D3-based visualization of Boston's MBTA transit system as a master's thesis project in 2014. Their published reflection includes a sentence that has become the canonical statement of the iteration-on-working-code principle: "Mockups and prototypes helped us formulate ideas, but nothing beat iterating on working code." The sentence is from 2014. It was about D3 development before LLMs. It applies more strongly now because the barrier to producing working code has dropped from hours to seconds.

// SLIDE 05 — THE AUDIT

THE AUDIT

Text.→Arrangement.→Color.→Lines.→Overall.
NARRATION

Between the first chart and the publishable chart is the audit. The audit is not intuition , "this doesn't feel right." It is a checklist applied systematically. Stephanie Evergreen and Ann Emery's 22-point data visualization checklist, which lives in the pantry as EvergreenDataVizChecklist.txt, organizes the audit into five categories. Chapter 14 walks the full checklist for project-level design review. This chapter uses the per-chart subset , the items most likely to fail on a Claude Code first output and most straightforward to fix.

// SLIDE 06 — COMMON FAILURES AND THE FOLLOW

COMMON FAILURES AND THE FOLLOW-UPS THAT FIX THEM

Auto-fit axis — d3.extent on quantitative scale
Wrong channel for data type — Hue ramp for quantitative variable
Wrong sort order — Source-file order on a categorical axis
Unnecessary label rotation — −30° rotation on horizontal-bar y-axis labels
NARRATION

Five failures recur across chart types. Each has a standard follow-up form. Y-axis auto-fit instead of zero baseline. Claude Code defaults to d3.extent(data) for the quantitative axis, which starts the axis at the minimum value in the dataset. For bar charts this violates proportional ink. The follow-up:

// SLIDE 07 — A CONSTITUTION FOR EVERY PROJE

A CONSTITUTION FOR EVERY PROJECT.

The four-move prompt works for a single chart. For a project with twenty charts, the four-move structure has an addition

A Constitution for Every Project — CLAUDE.md is the coding constitution. It holds project-wide standards that Claude Code should apply to every chart: D3 v
NARRATION

The four-move prompt works for a single chart. For a project with twenty charts, the four-move structure has an additional layer: the CLAUDE.md and DESIGN.md files from Chapter 00. CLAUDE.md is the coding constitution. It holds project-wide standards that Claude Code should apply to every chart: D3 version, file-naming convention, accessibility defaults, the requirement to add and ARIA labels. Anything you find yourself specifying in every "Constrain it" block is a candidate for CLAUDE.md.</p></div> </section><section class="slide" data-id="S08"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 08 — THE FULL PIPELINE, ONCE</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">THE FULL <span class="o">PIPELINE, ONCE.</span></h1> <div class="cbody"><div class="flow"><span class="chip">Stage 1 — Chapter 3 audit.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Stage 2 — Chapter 4 selection.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Stage 3 — Chapter 3 channel decomposition.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Stage 4 — This chapter's four-move prompt.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Stage 5 — Audit and iterate.</span></div></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>Here is the five-stage pipeline walked once through, with the humanitarian funding dataset from the chapter's opening. Stage 1 , Chapter 3 audit. Five rows. Sector: categorical, five values, no inherent order. Funding: quantitative, ratio scale, USD millions. One observation per category. Analyst's question: how are funds distributed? Reader's question: which sectors get the most and least? "Compared with what?" , sectors compared to each other, within-period. Relationship type: comparison.</p></div> </section><section class="slide" data-id="S09"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 09 — WHAT THIS CHANGES GOING FORWAR</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">WHAT THIS CHANGES <span class="o">GOING FORWARD.</span></h1> <div class="cbody"><p class="lead">Every chapter in Part II follows this pipeline. Each chart family — comparison, distribution, relationship, flow, spatia</p><div class="callout"><b>What This Changes Going Forward</b> — The pipeline is the constant. The channel decomposition belongs in the "Constrain it" block of every chart prompt, regar</div></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>Every chapter in Part II follows this pipeline. Each chart family , comparison, distribution, relationship, flow, spatial , adds chart-family-specific design rules to the "Constrain it" block. Chapter 6 adds the zero-baseline rule and the sort-by-value convention for bar charts. Later chapters add the IQR whisker rule for box plots, the log-scale-for-skewed-data convention for scatterplots, the rate-not-absolute-count rule for choropleths. The rules are additions to the framework you now have. They do not replace it. The pipeline is the constant.</p></div> </section><section class="slide" data-id="S10"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 10 — EXERCISES</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">EXERCISES<span class="o"></span></h1> <div class="cbody"><div class="flow"><span class="chip">Exercise 5.1 — Move identification.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Exercise 5.2 — Targeted follow-up writing.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Exercise 5.3 — Audit application.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Exercise 5.4 — Vague vs. four-move comparison.</span><span class="arw">→</span><span class="chip">Exercise 5.5 — Full pipeline, one chart.</span></div></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>Exercise 5.1 , Move identification. The opening case contains two prompts. For the second prompt, label each of the four moves explicitly. Identify which line or block constitutes Move 1, Move 2, Move 3, and Move 4. Then identify one thing the second prompt specifies that the first prompt leaves to Claude Code's default , and name what Claude Code's default would likely have been. Exercise 5.2 , Targeted follow-up writing.</p></div> </section><section class="slide" data-id="S11"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 11 — PROMPTS</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">PROMPTS<span class="o"></span></h1> <div class="cbody"><p class="lead">Use these prompts with Claude to generate interactive D3 v7 versions of the figures in this chapter. Each produces a sta</p><div class="callout"><b>Prompts</b> — Prerequisites: Load brutalist/CLAUDE.md and brutalist/DESIGN.md into your Claude project context before using these prom</div></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>Use these prompts with Claude to generate interactive D3 v7 versions of the figures in this chapter. Each produces a standalone HTML file you can open in a browser and modify freely. Prerequisites: Load brutalist/CLAUDE.md and brutalist/DESIGN.md into your Claude project context before using these prompts. They define the stack, naming conventions, color system, and typography the figures use.</p></div> </section><section class="slide" data-id="S12"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 12 — THESIS</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">THE CORE <span class="o">CLAIM.</span></h1> <div class="cbody"><div class="thesis">Use these prompts with Claude to generate interactive D3 v7 versions of the figures in this chapter. Each produces a sta</div><p class="lead">Prerequisites: Load brutalist/CLAUDE.md and brutalist/DESIGN.md into your Claude project context before using these prom</p></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>Use these prompts with Claude to generate interactive D3 v7 versions of the figures in this chapter. Each produces a standalone HTML file you can open in a browser and modify freely. Prerequisites: Load brutalist/CLAUDE.md and brutalist/DESIGN.md into your Claude project context before using these prompts. They define the stack, naming conventions, color system, and typography the figures use.</p></div> </section><section class="slide sdark" data-id="S13"> <div class="smeta"><span class="lbl">// SLIDE 13 — CLOSE</span><div class="sdiv"></div><span class="dot8"></span></div> <h1 class="stitle">ASK THE QUESTION. <span class="o">APPLY THE FRAMEWORK.</span></h1> <div class="cbody"><div class="close"><div class="close-tags"><span>THE FOUR-MOVE STRUCTURE</span><span>//</span><span>WHAT CLAUDE CODE DOES AND</span><span>//</span><span>THE MBTA LESSON</span></div><p class="sig">Brutalist D3 × Claude · Ch.5 · Chapter 6 — Working with Claude Code</p></div></div> <div class="ccap"><span class="ccap-tag">NARRATION</span><p>That is the framework. Brutalist D3 times Claude, chapter 5: Chapter 6 , Working with Claude Code. The patterns are now in place. Apply them.</p></div> </section></div> <div id="controls"><button class="ctrl" id="soundBtn" title="sound (m)">🔇</button></div> <div id="dots"></div> <div id="scount">01 / 13</div> <div U0dgS4k244UGFFjBDmC3hk19VHNRQCsTa1i+zE2Wbmy6G4mSpTVZ4MnAjgW9dUwdSZGB947vbn7FdtvXd8fz//NkxPcg0jJPHspMHP9d/9/3WpdqDuu0u2v1lkApwUCabyNyGksSabdzlSURSnh6dtySXyz1V31a8U7aJU13cfLuRmsqCqjRx0ZYpkpyX3tm1aIRUlDo0bIrPKtkT+rUsYYXMKohD6fCloN4eZEzK7izU4pzV3C2sVIi39tyIydUczF6R2mO+R2rxllOTf68//NkxPYemfJGXsmGfdwu/d37mgrac7IqYr+6BoZ1d4ZvtdrbDiHYDI2KxK6PpDFlItaLsF3fcRaW15u0pvUeT6/H99724WsMbBq8RLqH00ItLy0bCrL6VkEnvBs0mnuRPnfsoYm32kLwpWp+7UtBSdH8njMypv2thjE2YxJDamimHhp5O09YD0lWpAMq7qjl//NkxP4iIoo+XspG3eL0C+879Nn2PGK12E/DOefzDVud/X/ev/1/7v/8f5jkfVVIJKd6kfqlueS9stZLPepIYCQhUizqFYeg5E2yohJwsyEHGsggkcnj14lI2dNqYQMY3HJES9ZP8tqm0Xnb0sotms0NeRNZuVrvT9mhlFbcdOEmumcqm1ypp89ovE6dv1sj//NkxPgkgjZLHssMOdShoTDIcadJiqEtFQ+JQbIWMaAVPiDcGmyK9sd+tnr01Qv3JJZbYwAAoFb6EdnKkz81b6bKLU8+W9hPz07ThuTTo2AkqbUo0aNjCxhTcaxxgLPiwKM4oSRKQq9XClzXpKTX6JyOlL6lxnEgIca57yK71UxBTUUzLjEwMFVVVVVVVVVV//N0xOkhinottMJMPFVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVV//M0xP0UEh4xvimG0FVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVVV//MUxOcAAAP8AAAAAFVVVVVVVVVVVVVV//MUxPIAAANIAAAAAFVVVVVVVVVVVVVV"}; const N = 13; const slides=[...document.querySelectorAll('.slide')]; const dotsEl=document.getElementById('dots'); const progress=document.getElementById('progress'); const scount=document.getElementById('scount'); const soundBtn=document.getElementById('soundBtn'); let cur=0, soundOn=false, audioEl=null; slides.forEach((_,i)=>{const d=document.createElement('div');d.className='ndot'+(i===0?' active':'');d.onclick=()=>go(i);dotsEl.appendChild(d);}); function stop(){if(audioEl){audioEl.pause();audioEl=null;}} function play(i){stop();if(!soundOn)return;const src=AUDIO[slides[i].dataset.id];if(!src)return;const a=new Audio(src);audioEl=a;a.play().catch(()=>{});} function go(i){if(i<0||i>=N)return;slides[cur].classList.remove('active');dotsEl.children[cur].classList.remove('active');cur=i;slides[cur].classList.add('active');dotsEl.children[cur].classList.add('active');progress.style.width=((i+1)/N*100)+'%';scount.textContent=String(i+1).padStart(2,'0')+' / '+String(N).padStart(2,'0');location.hash=String(i+1);play(i);} soundBtn.onclick=()=>{soundOn=!soundOn;soundBtn.textContent=soundOn?'🔊':'🔇';if(soundOn)play(cur);else stop();}; document.addEventListener('keydown',e=>{if(e.key==='ArrowRight'||e.key===' '){e.preventDefault();go(cur+1);}if(e.key==='ArrowLeft')go(cur-1);if(e.key==='m')soundBtn.click();}); function initHash(){const h=parseInt(location.hash.replace('#',''));if(h>=1&&h<=N){go(h-1);}else{go(0);}} window.addEventListener('hashchange',()=>{const h=parseInt(location.hash.replace('#',''));if(h>=1&&h<=N&&h-1!==cur)go(h-1);}); initHash(); </script> </body></html>
